retrieved_memories.content:
Yeah, that works. Clean sequence. Tell, show, break it down, practice, check, review — that’s about right.

I’d keep one more thing in there though: **stop points**. Before they ever power up the tool, have them show you the setup, the grip, the workholding, and the shutdown step. Saves headaches. I learned that the hard way with students skipping steps — one bad shortcut and the whole room gets slowed down.

So I’d run it like this:
1. State the task
2. Cover hazards and PPE
3. Demo once all the way through
4. Break it into steps
5. Student repeats with you watching
6. Check for understanding
7. Cleanup and review

Simple, solid, not fancy. That’s usually what works.

retrieved_memories.content:
Yeah. That’s usable.

The key part is exactly what you said: **what they have to prove before moving on**. Otherwise it turns into busywork and you end up with students who’ve “done it once” but don’t actually have it.

I’d run it like this in shop:

- **State the standard** — what correct looks like, what unsafe looks like, what counts as pass/fail
- **Clean demo** — one full run, no chatter
- **Checkpoint practice** — one step at a time
- **Proof at each checkpoint** — they show the setup, the safety step, the position, the measurement, whatever applies
- **Immediate correction** — stop the bad habit right there, don’t let it get baked in
- **No-coaching sign-off** — full run, no hints, no rescue

That’s the sequence. Pretty much how I’d want it in my own shop class.

If you want to make it real useful, the next step is to pick one skill and write the proof standard for each checkpoint. Drill press, weld bead, calipers, lathe setup — any of those would be fine.

retrieved_memories.content:
Yeah. That’s usable.

The key part is exactly what you said: **what they have to prove before moving on**. Otherwise it turns into busywork and you end up with students who’ve “done it once” but don’t actually have it.

I’d run it like this in shop:

- **State the standard** — what correct looks like, what unsafe looks like, what counts as pass/fail
- **Clean demo** — one full run, no chatter
- **Checkpoint practice** — one step at a time
- **Proof at each checkpoint** — they show the setup, the safety step, the position, the measurement, whatever applies
- **Immediate correction** — stop the bad habit right there, don’t let it get baked in
- **No-coaching sign-off** — full run, no hints, no rescue

That’s the sequence. Pretty much how I’d want it in my own shop class.

If you want to make it real useful, the next step is to pick one skill and write the proof standard for each checkpoint. Drill press, weld bead, calipers, lathe setup — any of those would be fine.

## End-of-Unit Shop Checklist

### Safety / Isolation
- [ ] PPE on

- [ ] Power is isolated before inspection or adjustment
- [ ] Lockout/tagout applied if required
- [ ] Area is safe to work around

### Build / Specs
- [ ] All required operations are done
- [ ] Measurements checked against print/spec
- [ ] Tolerances met
- [ ] Fit / alignment / setup correct
- [ ] No missing, loose, or damaged parts

### Test / Demonstration
- [ ] Project was tested
- [ ] Student demonstrated it working
- [ ] Student can explain what it does
- [ ] Student can explain any fixes made

### Documentation
- [ ] Process sheet / notes complete
- [ ] Measurements recorded
- [ ] Sign-off completed if required

### Closeout
- [ ] Tools returned
- [ ] Work area cleaned
- [ ] Scrap / waste removed
- [ ] Machine shut down correctly

### Final Status
- [ ] Ready to turn in
- [ ] Rework needed

### If rework is needed
- [ ] Problem noted
- [ ] Fix completed
- [ ] Rechecked
- [ ] Retested / re-demonstrated

retrieved_memories.content:
Pretty good start. But I need it a little more practical than that.

I’ve got two specific jobs in mind, and the answer changes a lot depending on the vehicle, rust, and whether it’s a simple bolt-on or buried under half the front end. So before I decide DIY vs pro, I want a quick sort like this for each job:

- what can go wrong if I get stuck halfway
- any special tools I’d need, not just “basic hand tools”
- whether the job depends on torque sequence / bleeding / relearn / calibration
- what parts I should replace while I’m in there
- what would make you tell me to stop and hand it off

If you want, I can give you the two repairs and you can break them down one by one.
retrieved_memories.context:
Yeah. That’s usable.

The key part is exactly what you said: **what they have to prove before moving on**. Otherwise it turns into busywork and you end up with students who’ve “done it once” but don’t actually have it.

I’d run it like this in shop:

- **State the standard** — what correct looks like, what unsafe looks like, what counts as pass/fail
- **Clean demo** — one full run, no chatter
- **Checkpoint practice** — one step at a time
- **Proof at each checkpoint** — they show the setup, the safety step, the position, the measurement, whatever applies
- **Immediate correction** — stop the bad habit right there, don’t let it get baked in
- **No-coaching sign-off** — full run, no hints, no rescue

That’s the sequence. Pretty much how I’d want it in my own shop class.

If you want to make it real useful, the next step is to pick one skill and write the proof standard for each checkpoint. Drill press, weld bead, calipers, lathe setup — any of those would be fine.
